author: Stan1ey
title: The best(worst) course of HKUST?
comment_content:
What I expected:
Human language technology for processing text and spoken language. Fundamental machine learning, syntactic parsing, semantic interpretation, and context models, algorithms, and techniques. Applications include machine translation, web technologies, text mining, knowledge management, cognitive modeling, intelligent dialog systems, and computational linguistics.

What I have:
2019.01.31 1 Lecture Welcome; Introduction; Survey
2019.02.05 1 Holiday Lunar New Year
2019.02.07 1 Holiday Lunar New Year
2019.02.12 2 Lecture Impact of AI on ethics and society
2019.02.13 2 Lecture Does God play dice? Assumptions: scientific method, hypotheses, models, learning, probability; linguistic relativism and the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis; inductive bias, language bias, search bias; the great cycle of intelligence [at tutorial]
2019.02.14 2 Lecture Languages of the world
Administrivia (honor statement, HKUST classroom conduct)
2019.02.19 3 Lecture Learning to translate: engineering, social, and scientific motivations
2019.02.20 3 Lecture "It's all Chinese to me": linguistic complexity; challenges in modeling translation [at tutorial]
2019.02.21 3 Lecture Is machine translation intelligent? Interactive simulation [at tutorial]
2019.02.26 4 Lecture Evaluating translation quality: alignment; aligning semantic frames: Interactive exercise
2019.02.27 4 Lecture Evaluating translation quality: HMEANT [at tutorial]
2019.02.28 4 Lecture Evaluating translation quality: MEANT
2019.03.05 5 Lecture Evaluating translation quality: semantic role labeling (SRL), case frames, semantic frames, predicate-argument structure
2019.03.06 5 Lecture Automatic semantic role labeling (ASRL) [at tutorial]
2019.03.07 5 Lecture Implementing a feedforward neural network based part-of-speech tagger Assignment 1 due 2019.03.15 23:59; context-independent POS tagging
2019.03.12 6 Lecture I/O representations for feedforward networks; context-dependent POS tagging
2019.03.13 6 Tutorial Basic probability theory; conditional probabilities; Bayes' theorem
2019.03.14 6 Lecture Example-based, instance-based, memory-based, case-based, analogy-based, lazy learning for classification; translation via nearest neighbors (NN); k-NN; weighted k-NN
2019.03.19 7 Exam Midterm (closed book; handwritten notebook only)
2019.03.21 7 Lecture Midterm review; machine translation techniques
2019.03.26 8 Lecture Learning vs performance components in machine learning; supervised learning; Word sense disambiguation; lexical choice; example-based prediction models; nearest neighbor classifiers; similarity metrics; kNN classifiers
2019.03.28 8 Lecture Exploring different feedforward neural network architectures for POS tagging; model design following scientific method for machine learning in practice Assignment 2 due 2019.04.10 23:59; AI ethics
2019.04.02 9 Lecture Naive Bayes classifiers for WSD and lexical choice
2019.04.04 9 Lecture Modern approaches to SRL; corporate responsibility in AI
2019.04.09 10 Lecture Implementing chunkers and shallow parsers via IOBES tagging plus a POS tagger Assignment 3 due 2019.05.10 23:59
2019.04.11 10 Lecture Chunking via IOBES representations; shallow bracketing
2019.04.16 11 Lecture Shallow syntactic parsing; shallow semantic parsing; language bias of IOBES representations, bags of words, and one-hot representations
2019.04.18 11 Lecture Introduction to word embeddings
2019.04.23 11 Lecture Vector space models; classic word vector approaches
2019.04.25 11 Lecture Learning word embeddings via prediction tasks; skip-grams; word2vec; Assignment 3 discussion
2019.04.30 12 Lecture Recursive autoencoders (RAEs) and recursive auto-associative memories (RAAM); learning word embeddings by making RAEs predict; AI ethics in China; utilitarian and consequentialist ethics; Asilomar AI principles
2019.05.02 12 Lecture Context-free grammars (CFGs); generative vs parsing models; top-down vs bottom-up parsing; dynamic programming based chart parsing; Cocke-Kasami-Younger (CKY) parsing
2019.05.07 13 Lecture From CFGs to ITGs (monolingual vs bilingual modeling); how bilingual conditions make grammar induction easier; the mystery of the magic number 4 in semantic frames; simple and full syntax-directed transduction grammars (SDTGs); introductioon to inversion transduction grammars (ITGs); tree vs matrix constituent alignment visualizations
2019.05.08 13 Lecture ITG characteristics; stochastic ITGs; polynomial-time transduction and learning; resolving the mystery of the magic number 4
2019.05.09 13 Lecture From RAAM to TRAAM (transduction RAAM); recursive neural network realizations of ITGs; a self-learning rap battle bot

The real content:
AI ethics, AI ethics and AI ethics with some small proportion of AI theory.
If you want to learn RNN, back propagation, gradient decent or Markov chain etc. Congratulation, please take another AI course, you won't learn anything from these course other from AI ethics or personal achievement of Dekai. Well done!

However, if you want to use professor's achievement to masturbate. Good! The best course of UST! Please enroll it at once.
comment_teaching:
Dekai WU. Umm, A good-looking man. Long hair, beard and well-taste dressing. Besides, he is probably the only Chinese who can articulate English accurately.

If you want to enjoy his pronunciation, this is the best course of UST.

Okay, let's back to real performance.

These two articles are written by me. These two articles has already contains most of my thought about this course.

Sorry foreigners. Originally I want to use the machine learning for translation which should be taught in this course to translate those paragraphs. However, as I haven't learnt anything, this become impossible.  :(
comment_grading:
Well. I don't know what should I graded in this section because I don't know my final score and the score of assignment 3 and assignment 1. What I know is I get a B eventually.

For reference:
I got full with 5%bonus in assignment 2 and get 80% in midterm exam.

There is one thing to remind all of you who want to take this course. Please write the correct name of Dekai, Wu in the midterm and final exam. Otherwise, your paper won't be graded or mark deduction.

Besides, midterm will mainly cover what his said during the lecture instead of lecture slide. You can bring hand written note  during exam. So, you should drop note every lectures or steal your friend hand written note directly or force him to help you drop note.

Final will mainly cover what he don't teach during lecture. Believe in your luck.
緣分到了，你自然會識做份卷。
comment_workload:
The most heaviest workload course in HKUST. Even heavier than all LANG courses or SOSC and HUMA common core. There a three assignments.
The first assignment will waste you about 5 days to complete.
The second assignment will waste you about a week to complete it.
After the deadline of second assignment, the third assignment will release. The workload is three times of the second assignment, which mean you have to use about 3 weeks to complete a single assignment individually.
Even worse, there are many system maintenance. Your assignment progress will force to stop during maintenance. Eventually, the time for you to complete the assignment will reduce.
Another serious issue is an assignment will divide to three parts. Each part will release each week, which mean you cannot finish the next part before it release. So, you won't be able to plan finishing all parts during its release.
As assignment 3 is released at the last of the semester. you will be very busy.

title: Victorization: The Process of Achieving Victor(y)
comment_content:
Content is actually an acceptable introduction to the modern field of Natural Language Processing - but the PPTs were not very organized, nor could they effectively explain the intuition behind each concept, which made it rather hard to comprehend. The vast amount of content also made it incredibly hard to revise (or even understand).

At any rate, the topics are as follows:
Text Processing
- Tokenization and the BPE Encoder
- Edit Distance and Text Alignment (featuring Dynamic Programming)
N-Gram Language Model
- Bigrams and Trigrams
- Perplexity
- Smoothing (Add-1 and Kneser-Ney)
Text Classification
- Sentiment Analysis (Naive Bayes)
- Bag of Words
- Precision, Recall, and F1
- Significance and Bootstrapping
Word Representation Learning
- Word Semantic Meanings
- Term-Document/Term-Term Matrices (COMP 4321 material)
- Similarity Measures (Cosine and Inner product)
- Word2Vec Embedding
Neural Networks and Neural Language Models
- Neural Units
- Activation Functions (ReLU, Sigmoid, tanh)
- Perceptrons and Multi-layer Perceptrons
- Backpropagation and Computational Graphs
RNN and Transformer Models
- Simple RNN
- Special Units: LSTM, GRU
- Sequence Learning and Sequence2Sequence
- Encoder/Decoder Architecture
- Beam Search
- Attention (Cross- and Self-Attention)
- Transformer Architecture
Chatbots and Dialogue Systems
- PoS tagging
- Historical Perspectives: ELIZA
- Frame-Based Dialogue Architecture
- Dialogue-State Architecture
- Ethical Considerations
Syntactic Parsing
- Context-Free Grammars (CFGs)
- Probabilistic CFGs
- Chomsky Normal Form
- Dependency Parse Trees and Maximum Spanning Trees
Pretrained Language Models (Advanced)
- Natural Language Generation
- Realization and (De)lexicalization
- TransformerXL and XLnet
- Prompt Tuning

The contents actually overlap slightly with a multitude of other COMP courses - COMP 2211, COMP 4211, COMP 4321 are all at least somewhat relevant if taken in the same semester.

Although I have listed a lot of content, I don't think I have actually learnt much through the course - I understood neural networks from COMP 2211, a bit of RNN/Transformer from the COMP 4211 slides, and the things about information retrieval from COMP 4321.

Would recommend 3Blue1Brown's introductory videos about Transformers/ChatGPT - those are pretty good for illustrating what the abstract Math actually means.
comment_teaching:
Given that Prof Wei is a new Prof, I can excuse his teaching quality to a certain degree. I also appreciated his prompt upload for the RVC recordings, which is quite rare. However, I must say there is quite a lot of room for improvement, especially regarding his delivery and elaboration.

While he may rarely elaborate on the concepts on the slides, he simply read out the Math equations (or the content on the slides) verbatim in a flat tone. Coupled with the 9am Friday lectures, this definitely did not help me understand what is going on.

The slides themselves do have a lot of content, but they were unable to deliver much value since most of them are only Math equations without much elaboration. The motivations behind much of the content are skipped, and I have no idea how some of the equations appeared, nor the conceptual understanding behind it.

D for at least (probably) being better than Dekai, but the teaching quality could definitely be worked on.
comment_grading:
The most disastrous part of the course is definitely the assessment and grading.

The grading scheme is as follows:
30% Written Assignments (15% each)
20% Midterm Exam
20% Course Project
30% Final Exam

Somehow, each and every part of this grading scheme went wrong.

The written assignments were the least disastrous parts of the entire assessment scheme, although there were trivial in the sense that much of what is asked of you should really be done by a computer instead of being done by hand. Assignment 1 had you count bigrams and calculating probabilities by hand - extremely tedious and could be done by computers. Assignment 2 was worse - you had to calculate multiple matrix products, which could definitely be done better by a computer. In fact, most of us used Numpy to do assignment 2, which clearly showed that the assignments were of limited utility.

The midterm exam was a landmark in the history of "worst midterm exams". It was a direct copy of Assignment 1 (with a few numerical tweaks), with the relatively more difficult question completely deleted. The midterm was scheduled for an hour - most people walked out after half an hour. The low difficulty ended up creating one of the most impressive box plots I've seen - the median for the midterm was 100.

Let me repeat: The midterm exam of a 4000-level COMP course has a median of 100. That is perhaps one of the most impressive things in my 3 years of study here. I've attached the box plot for your reference (I have set the what-if score to 0).

The course project is another example of poor assessment design. 20 marks were awarded for simply running the given code. Another 50 to 60 marks are for modifying 1-2 lines of code. Although the project did give us some experience with NLP in Python, what we learnt was effectively absolutely nothing - given that the code was given, and none of the libraries used were ever explained in any depth whatsoever.

All of the above (constituting 70% of the course marks) had a median of 100. This means that 50% of all students (roughly) had full marks before the final. This makes it impossible to distribute grades. So what does Prof Wei do?

He sets the most insane final exam that I have had the misfortune of sitting through. At least for COMP 3021 (Prof Shen version), the questions were hard but definitely conceptually interesting. The paper was fair as it at least examined how clear your concepts are. Prof Wei's paper occupied the other end of the difficulty spectrum, falling squarely into the field of brutally and unnecessarily hard due to sheer tedium. The final featured 8 questions, and was ~40 pages long since the paper was double-side printed. The questions were designed to be tedious and impossible to complete in the allotted period of time. One of the questions asked us to dry run the BPE algorithm 8 times. Another asked us to multiply 4x4 matrices together, manually apply softmax to it, and multiply it with another matrix again. Repeat these steps 3-4 times with minor modifications, and you will have gained a total of 15 marks out of 100, despite the number of numerical calculations required. Another 15 mark question required you to write down the pseudocode for Naive Bayes twice with minor modifications, and dry run it on 5 sentences. The level of number crunching required in the exam was extraordinary, and completely irrelevant to the course aims. Some of the questions also required you to recite some useless definitions (I don't think anyone needs to know the definition of a language model, nor do they need to recite the psuedocode of the Naive Bayes classifier). The exam felt like a useless trivia memorization test and a human calculation test stapled together in 20 sheets of paper - an extremely poor reflection of our ability.

Needless to say, I am deeply disappointed by the course grading scheme.

"Fun" notes:
I heard at least half the hall swear at the exam paper during the final (that includes me). Someone shouted "Professor, do you hate us?" at the end of the paper. It was one of the loudest exams I've sat through - mainly because everyone is swearing to some degree.

I have attached the estimated LQs and UQs, as well as the official mean and SD.

Update post-solution release: I am literally speechless.
- Doing 3 4x4 matrix multiplications: 1/100;
- A 4-page solution: 3/100;
- Making a 27x27 table with different probabilities: 2/100.
Well done!

I am incensed by the released solution: Prof Wei didn't even bother typing out any of the definition questions. For the 4x4 matrices he used Numpy (when we could not). This made me question whether he actually did his paper after setting it. Well done!!
comment_workload:
The workload for this course is (mercifully) light, but given the disastrous state of the grading scheme, this really doesn't help at all.

The assignments and the course project only took 1-2 days to complete, and the midterm... was not meaningful to revise for.

The final took me 2-3 days to fully catch up with the content. Even then, most of my revised materials are useless since the exam was about being a human calculator.

author: m****6
title: Take this course if you're a human computer
comment_content:
The slides are nothing but a pot of mess. Just random words and equations scattered everywhere.

Topics are fine, but the content are not coherent or organised in any sense. The following topics are not listed in the teaching order:
- Text Processing (Words and Corpora, Tokenization, Alignments, Edit Distance)
- N-Gram Language Model
- Text Classification
- Word Representation Learning
- Sequence Labelling for Parts of Speech and Named Entities
- Neural Networks and Neural Language Models
- RNN and Transformer Models
- Pretrained Language Models (Basic)
- Sequence-to-Sequence Models and Machine Translation
- Question Answering and Information Retrieval
- Chatbots and Dialogue Systems
- Constituency Parsing
- Dependency Parsing
- Pretrained Language Models (Advanced)
- Prompting and Instruct Tuning
comment_teaching:
Like 9 out of 10 other UST professor, he just speaks in the usual monotonic voice to simply read out the slides during lectures.

The reason I didn't gave him a E is because he always releases the lecture recording on Canvas.

Always makes mistakes in assignment and exam questions -_-
At least interrupted us three times in the final exam for various clarification / fixing the questions.
comment_grading:
Well what can I say for this? See the earlier post if you want to know the full story. Long story short, the entire grading policy simply could not differentiate students by their understanding on the course material.

Grading scheme: (Mean/LQ/Median/UQ)
15% Assignment 1 (90.8/97/98/100)
15% Assignment 2 (88.11/85/100/100)
20% Project (83.21/90/90/100)
20% Midterm (97.74/96.75/100/100)
30% Final (42.17/37/42.5/51)

As you can see, this is a sh*tty distribution. Everyone just get 100 in every single part of the course except in the final exam where the prof decides to screw us all over. Made a careless mistake in your assignment? Say goodbye to your A/B range.

The "project" is simply running a given python script and write several lines of code yourself, definitely not the PA kind of work.

The final exam is so legendary that I will never forget. The moment I went into the exam venue and sit down at the chair, I was surprised by the sheer thickness of the exam paper which is supposed for a 3-hour examination. Turns out it was double-sided and ends up with like 40 pages in total, which already seem quite dramatic for a 3-hour exam.

The content of the exam is purely evil and designed to screw us over. Have you tried doing a matrix multiplication? Then you know how annoying it is to do that for higher dimension matrix such as 4x4. Guess what? You need to do it multiple times just for a single sub-question for 5 marks in the scaled dot-product attention part. Basically you need to do a 4x4 x 4x4, then apply softmax, and do a 4x4 x 4x4 again! At this point, you have done (7+4+7)*16 = 288 add/multiply and applied the exponential functions for 20 times! Congrats, you earned yourself 3 marks now! Now, do it for a few more times for the other sub-questions ^_^

You think that is it? Try BPE for ~15-20 characters and 5 lines of text and run it for 8 iterations and that would grant you another 3 points ^_^

Did you recite the pseudo-code for multiple algorithms such as graph-based dependency parsing and apply it to 15 nodes interconnecting with 4-5 arrows with one another that you can barely see which arrow is which one? Or did you recite that Naive Bayes classifier algorithm in pseudo-code? No? Too bad, 30 marks gone.

Don't want to handle codes? No problem, let's circle back to math, just draw a SIMPLE computational graph for a 2-2-1 MLP structure with a maximum of two items for each inter-step (e.g. v1 = w1x1 + w2x2 + b1 would need to split into 4 nodes: a=w1x1, b=w2x2, c=a+b, d=c+b1, imagine how many nodes you would generate for the entire MLP structure with just a simple 2-2-1, just imagine you are a CPU running assembly code), and write the respective partial differentiation for like 30 nodes. For this you would get 2 points!

The entire lecture theater is just filled with gasps, sighs and non-stop cursing. To be honest I have no idea what the final grading will be like, or what will happen next. At this point I don't think there's anything they can do to justify what they did to us in the final exam.
comment_workload:
Light workload and you learn nothing from them.
Only 2 assignment and a project which constitutes 35% of your final grade which everyone gets a full mark from. Absolutely no pressure ^_^
1 midterm and 1 final, just regular stuff ^_^

author: Bits
title: Ilya Sutskever will fail this final.
comment_content:
Topic is good, I actually learned something (by myself).
However, trying to comprehend the information conveyed in the slides felt like solving a sudoku puzzle. The slides did not directly express the key points and concepts. Instead, I had to use logical reasoning and deduction to figure out what the slides were attempting to communicate. The slides lacked clarity and straightforward explanations, requiring significant mental effort to extract the underlying meaning.

```

prof.read(("OK").join(content.splitlines()))
```

comment_grading:
The final exam is like a inverse-turing test. If you pass the exam, you are not a human, you are a robot. Because it's nothing about knowledge, it's just a huge amount of calculation. No one can do this much of calculation by hand in 3 hours.

I've never experienced this kind of final exam in my whole life. So stupid.
comment_workload:
Light, but we learned nothing from the assignments. We need more exercises! More PyTorch coding!!!

Overall, I'd say the instructor was so lazy. He didn't take this course seriously at all, and this was very disrespectful to all the students who took his course :(

author: 2042XXXX
title: if (professor == dekai) { students->course.drop();}
comment_content:
The content of this course is supposed to be rich, giving students ideas what NLP is.

There are 3 assignments, midterm and final in the course.

The assignment in this course is full of idealism that Dekai wishes the TA to design yet the TA has no time to do it at all. I personally felt bad for the TA as
1. The TA don't even have any materials sent by Dekai
2. The TA is asked by Dekai to asked question that he never mention and define in class (Search Bias), so basically the TA doesn't know what does it mean either.

All in all, there are 3 assignments in this course for this semester, in which assignment 1 was a easy and basic one, and I already get lost in assignment 2 and 3 as I have no way of catching up and understanding his ideas.

As a sidenote, the assignment 3 was broken up into Part A, B and C and suppose we would hand in part by part. This caused a lot of confusion to the class at first and the deadline was unclear at the first.

The Midterm consists of discussion in class about ethnics and some basic hazards he thinks AI could do to humanity (not strictly related to NLP but that's his style), some POS tagging which he spend only one lecture and giving no exercise to us and suppose we will achieve 100% marks from that after weeks of introduction. Seriously is this set up to fail us? I don't know, but most of us can't even understand the POS tag basics during that lecture.

Let's be clear that I did not study for the final because I have already given up the course by that point. It consists of a lot of pseudo programming using C++ syntax and also some question yet again discussed in class.

All exams are open handwritten notes (although the definition of "handwritten notes" is not clearly defined by the professor and TA)
comment_teaching:
Although I gave the grade E here, but let's admire him for one of the very few professor that in UST who teaches with passion and energy. I do think he really does a great job of enhancing the atmosphere of the classroom.

These traits, however, is neglected by how unclear, messy, unstructured and unreleased lecture notes to further help understanding to the deep and wide knowledge I wish the course provides to me.

I wish he knew that not all and actually most of his students are not genius and would like step by step approach to have a better introduction to this matter. While I'm okay with his introduction of the backgrounds, his content and difficulty skyrocketed as he is skipping many ideas without even explain it slowly, and not having any contents on what he's trying to say in class just made everything worse and worse.
comment_grading:
Dekai never gave any clear definition of the grading scheme of the course, but I got C-. He does say you will get guarantee grading on achieving certain point, but I saw some reviews already include this so I am not bothering to repeat here.

Let just say it is relatively good as I didn't do 75% of assignment 3, and most things I wrote in the final, I actually have no ideas what I am doing. I was expecting to get an D or F, but C- was really unexpected.
comment_workload:
Very heavy. with assignments released and due in a week, many students found it hard to complete with in time. He, however is willing to extend the deadline often as quite clearly the class has stated that we all have no idea what you homework is all about.

And lastly to those who read all of my grumble till to this very end, thank you and please, if you want some introduction to NLP, don't take this course. It is not worth your time to waste here.

author: A0
title: DO NOT TAKE THIS COURSE!
comment_content:
DO NOT TAKE THIS COURSE!

Do not take this course if you want to learn natural language processing. Go to MIT / Standford / Coursera / Udemy instead.

What I learnt:

1. How to use Dekai's forum.
We spent 2 lectures to register our account in the forum.
And spent 2 lectures doing discussions on the forum.

2. How to play Dekai's translation game.
We spend 2 lectures playing his translation game.
I understand that this game teaches us about the difficulty of language translation, but sorry, playing the game for two lessons feels like a waste of time.

3. How to simulate Dijkstra Algorithm with washers and strings
comment_teaching:
DO NOT TAKE THIS COURSE!

Well, this semester's teaching is done through ZOOM so you won't have the problem of missing a lecture or write notes since you can just re-watch the lecture recordings.
So the problem of not giving out lecture notes mentioned in previous reviews would not be as big of a problem than normal face to face lectures.
But it would be better if Dekai can released the lecture notes since without the lecture notes, I needed to type the lecture notes myself in a word document during the lecture, which is annoying.
Some students asked Dekai during lecture to release the lecture notes, because the equations are quite complicated, he agreed, but never did.

His English is okay, but it is hard to understand the content, since some of the content is difficult such as the Hidden Markov Model, CKY parsing, ITG hypothesis...
comment_grading:
DO NOT TAKE THIS COURSE!

Grade weighting
Exams 0% (due to university coronavirus measures)
Pop quizzes ~10%
Class participation ~15%
Forum participation ~10%
Assignments ~65%

My grades for pop quiz:
I don't know, there were no pop quizzes in the whole semester. And Dekai didn't mention where the points from pop quiz is allocated to. So it is a mystery.

My grades for assignment:
Assignment 1: 100/100 (Some students obtained 5 more points for bonus)
Assignment 2: 100/100
Assignment 3: 100/100
Assignment 4: 100/100

My grades for class participation:
I don't know my grades for class participation, because Dekai never released the grades for this part.
I would just guess that I get a mean mark for this part, since I would consider my participation in the class would be normal.
But still, I don't know how this part is graded because a clear marking scheme for class participation is not there for us to see.
So if he wants, he can just gives you bad grade just because you look ugly, or gives you good grade, if you kept on liking his boots.


My grades for forum participation:
I don't know my grades for forum participation, because Dekai never released the grades for this part.
But I would just guess that I got a pretty good grade for forum participation, since I would say that I was quite active in the forum, answering questions and asking questions.
Again, no one knows how this part is graded, since he never released the marking scheme for this part.

My grade: B+
Overall grading was not good.
Only 13 students enroll in this class, and in the first lecture, he said he was quite lenient for giving A range grade that he usually need to beg ARR for allowing him to give out more A ranges than usual.
So I figured that for grading 13 students in a curve, a lenient A range, full marks assignment, okay class participation and good forum participation, then I would expect an A-. But he gives B+ for me, lower then I expected.

With no grading detail and mark release for class and forum participation, weighting percentage (with the disappearing pop quiz). The grading was not transparent at all. So expect your final grade to be marked subjectively.
comment_workload:
DO NOT TAKE THIS COURSE!

Mid-term/Final:
No mid term and final for this semester, so you don't need to memorize how Dekai Wu is spelt. (To be honest, I think every CS student in HKUST knows his name since he was quite infamous for his teaching, just look at the other reviews)

Assignments:
Assignments are due in one week ~ 1.5 weeks, and it is quite hard to finish them in time especially if you are year 4 with FYP or took a lot of courses in that semester.

Assignment 1: Dijkstra Anagram
Assignment requirement is to write a program to do Dijkstra algorithm for generating anagram.
This first assignment is okay, not too difficult.
But for getting the 5 points bonus it is quite hard, since your program need to run fast enough within 20s for 14 character length word, which required you to use some sort of pruning to achieve that efficiency.

Assignment 2: HMM
Assignment requires to write a program for Hidden Markov Model backward forward algorithm.
This assignment is hard, since the concept and equations of backward and forward algorithm and HMM is quite difficult and complicated. So you need to spend a lot of time to understand it.
And with the complicated equations, debugging takes a long time.

Assignment 3: Feedforward POS Tagger
Assignment requirement is to loop through the scientific method for feed forward part of speech tagger.
This assignment is easy in terms of coding, since for every hypothesis, just a few lines of code is needed to be changed.
The annoying part is that you would need to write observations of the training set, description of hypothesis, and do error analysis for each hypothesis.
And because I didn't know whether the answers are correct or not, I tried to write as long as possible for the description and explanation, which I used a lot of time to do. I don't know whether it is necessary to write so long, but in the end I got 100/100

Assignment 4: RNN
Assignment requirement: create bidirectional RNN, LSTM, and bidirectional LSTM model for tagging part of speech for the training set with the starting code of RNN.
I would say this is the most useless and annoying assignment because the machine learning framework used in this assignment is LEGO, which is developed by Dekai and his TAs.
Why the hell would you ask the students to use C++ and your own LEGO machine learning framework, but not python with state-of-the-art machine learning framework such as pyTorch or Tensorflow?
Not to mention that because the framework is developed by Dekai and his TAs, there are no documentation that you can refer to understand how LEGO actually works.
So you would spend a lot of time trying to understand the starting code with the comments, do trial and error to understand how different function works and the output of different variables.
Basically, this assignment teaches you to use LEGO machine learning framework, which you would never ever use again in your whole life. (Unless you decide to do postgrad and become his TA)

author: Luoshu
title: I have no idea what the hell dekai is talking about.
comment_content:
The course title about the course is "natural language processing". However, I have no idea what the professor is talking about.

The course material related to the NLP is taught after the midterm exam. Before the midterm, it is all about some historical staff. (In fact, I am not a fan in history and humanity. I cannot remember what exactly it is taught.)
comment_teaching:
Normally, in one lecture, the professor will talk something about himself or the AI ethics (such as some buzz word about him being elected to some council). Afterwards, he may deliver some jibber jabber about the language history or some other boring staff.

To be honest, at the first two weeks of the semester, I think it was my problem that I cannot understand the content and the purpose of his teaching. However, after several weeks, when I realized that the teaching is not understandable at all.

Ultimately, if you are really a huge fan of painful experience (or "抖M" in Chinese), I strongly recommend you to take this course.
comment_grading:
To my great surprise, the grading is astonishingly good. For your information, the weight of assignments are very high, so please make sure you complete all assignments in a serious attitude.

After checking my final exam, I have scored below the mean score. However, I am still able to obtain an A- grade. Anyway, this is a course that you don't have any idea about the grading detail.
comment_workload: There are 5 assignments in total. Due to the fact that I need to count on those assignments, I need to finish them in a deep and complete way. In fact, I have no idea how these will affect me. Therefore, all I can do is to write my report as long as possible.

author: Richard Blažek
comment_content:
Covers everything about natural language processing...
Segmentation (regular expressions, tokenization, lemmatization, normalization, byte-pair encoding), n-gram (with smoothing and interpolation), text classification, logistic regression, neural networks, backpropagation, convolutional neural networks, recurrent neural networks, transformer architecture, word encodings (word2vec etc.), machine translation, chat assistant systems and information retrieval.
comment_teaching:
Teacher is okay, but most people didn't attend the lectures (like 3 people attended an average lecture, even though there were around 20-30 students). I regret not attending them though, because I would certainly get a better grade on the final exam if I did.

The teacher has a habit of saying "okay" more than once per minute, like after every other sentence.
comment_grading: The final exam was quite hard as there was a lot of material. The midterm wasn't hard, but there was not enough time to actually do all the questions (it was worth skipping some time-consuming questions and start with the quick ones). Assignments and project were not that hard and they were graded fairly.
comment_workload: There are two assignments and one project. The assignments are very similar to the exam (exam is the assignment plus a few extra questions), so by doing them you also prepare for the exam in large part. For the project, there were detailed instructions for what you have to do, so it didn't take much ingenuity to do it, you just follow the steps.

author: gossipgirl
title: Weird course
comment_content:
Content covers the following topics:
1. Bigram models
2. Anagramming
3. HMM
4. IBM Models for Machine Translation
5. ITG Models for Machine Translation
6. CFG/PCFG/CYK Algorithm for parsing

The content is quite old. Agreed that some of these are important topics in this field but I expected an introduction to Neural MT or something.
comment_teaching:
I didn't like the teaching whatsoever. People say he's very passionate about the field etc etc. Agreed, but while teaching a course there should be a solid structure of content and way to assess students. 12 weeks of classes feel like a blur when you heard a lot of things but don't quite understand them too well.

There are no lecture notes as such. In class, a pdf of his book (should have been printed 3 years ago)  is used an then a printout is given before the exams. This makes it very difficult to take good notes and clear doubts during the semester.
comment_grading:
Dekai's grading scheme is very flexible and changes almost every year he teaches. He doesn't tell us the final scheme he used but a rough idea is
5% participation
50% assignments
20% midterm
25% final

Depending on how many assignments he gives, he adjusts the weight of the exams. He originally planned to teach scheme but due to lack of time, gave only 1 programming assignment and 1 written assignment. Also, he doesn't release the score of the final. (Yes, quite a few unknowns in the course)

I would say the final grade isn't that bad. His midterm is very easy and scoring. Final is definitely a bit harder. A range should not be very difficult.

author: Hung Ching Hei
comment_content: The amount of content is very large, but the instructor will tell you the scope of the exams, so you don't need to revise all the notes
comment_teaching: Since the instructor is just reading the ppt, it can be quite boring. But the instructor is willing to answer your email and questions. So please don't hesitate to email him if you got a question.
comment_grading:
Assignments (30%)
Course Project (20%)
Mid-term Exam (20%)
Final Exam (30%)
For assignment, project and mid term exam, you can say they are extremely easy, the mid term exam is even easier than the assignment. So final exam become the one that decide your grade, which need you to calculate the same stuff for like 4 to 5 times just for 7-8 marks, the whole exam is like this(just like others says-- inverse turing test). Please make sure you are well prepared for the final exam
comment_workload:
2 assignments
1 individual project
mid term and final exam
Not very large workload since the assignments and project are very easy to complete

author: readalot
title: old fashioned contents
comment_content:
The content was very old because he did not handle with deep learning such as Neural MT.

The content covered the following topics:
- Bigram models
- Anagramming
- HMM
- IBM / ITG Models for  Machine Translation
- CFG/PCFG/CYK Algorithm for parsing
comment_teaching: He was passionate but I don't like his teaching style. He did not share his lecture note such as pdf.
comment_grading:
He did not said the final scheme clearly, but said a rough idea
- 5% participation
- 50% assignments
- 20% midterm
- 25% final

A midterm was easy and scoring. A final was felt a bit harder.

author: RZ
comment_content: The content was interesting. But he didn't have time to address most of them, cause it was easy for us to start chatting. The content started from the history to some algorithm.
comment_teaching: Teaching was fair and sometimes interesting. But Dekai never uploads the slides so it's always hard for me to follow in most of the time.
comment_grading:
Absolute grade:
Participation
Tutorial participation
Forum participation (He has set up a forum for this course)
Assignments
In-class quiz
comment_workload: Workload was fair but the ddl was always tight. And it always costs much time to think about the right way to solve the problems. Assignment is released on his forum without noticing you on canvas, so make sure to check the forum frequently.

author: Frederica
comment_content: He gives an interesting perspective on Natural Language Processing as he is one of the ground-breaking researchers on translation. You will learn a lot about the Chinese <-> English translation.
comment_teaching: You will learn lots of theory as with every CS class in HKUST and some functional programming. He's a great teacher and his class is secretly a philosophy class as well. He encourages students to take NLP from other professors too if given the chance because he believes that each professor who teaches NLP gives a different perspective on it. It is not as hard to fall asleep in his class because he has quite a personality.
comment_grading:
In terms of grading, the breakdown for his class is:
85%         A
75% B
65% C
55% D

Midterm exam ~20%
Final exam ~25%
Participation ~5%
Assignments ~50%

Exams are not open book.
